key_insight: Thermal spray coatings can be engineered at the micro- to nanoscale to reduce interfacial losses and improve durability in batteries, fuel cells, electrolyzers, and solid oxide electrochemical systems.
engineering_relevance: By tuning spray process, porosity, phase distribution, and interfacial chemistry, engineers can create functional coatings that improve ionic/electronic transport, lower resistance, and extend device lifetime.
method: Reviewed major thermal spray routes: atmospheric plasma spray, suspension plasma spray, high-velocity oxy-fuel, and cold spray.
Mapped process parameters to coating microstructure, porosity, phase evolution, and interface chemistry using a process-structure-property framework.
Compared design principles across batteries, PEM/alkaline fuel cells, and SOFC/SOEC systems to identify transferable coating strategies.
evidence_signal: The paper is a review synthesis, drawing on existing studies to compare process-structure-property relationships rather than reporting new experimental datasets.
watch_item: Performance gains depend strongly on controlling defects, non-equilibrium phase changes, and transport limits, so scale-up and reproducibility remain challenging.
paper_title: Thermal spray coatings in batteries, electrolyzers and solid oxide electrochemical systems
